steps.title: Identify the key fact driving the prediction
steps.content: Common ravens can spot finch nests near the road far more easily than nests deep in the woodland. Because ravens eat finch eggs, easier spotting means more predation. Any logical prediction must follow from this visibility difference.

steps.title: Test each choice against the predation logic
steps.content:
The correct choice must connect easier spotting near the road to a higher egg-loss rate for those nests.
  • Choice A — 'finch eggs will make up about the same percentage of common ravens' diet as they did before the road was built.': predicts no change in predation, contradicting the new visibility advantage ravens gained from the road. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the number of finches building nests near the road will gradually increase as the finches adapt to the presence of the common ravens.': predicts finches will move toward a more dangerous area — illogical given the increased predation risk there. ✗
  • Choice C — 'finches nesting near the road will lose eggs to common ravens at a higher rate than will finches nesting far from the road.': directly follows from ravens spotting road-side nests more easily — higher visibility leads to higher predation. ✓
  • Choice D — 'finches nesting far from the road will lay fewer eggs per nest than will finches nesting near the road.': addresses egg-laying quantity in woodland nests, a factor not supported by anything in the passage. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
